hero.tagline: The physical body - links, joints, and the math that moves them
hero.intro: Robots are made of bodies. Before perception, planning, or control mean anything, you need to know what the robot is shaped like and how it can move. This branch covers the basics: rigid bodies, joints, actuators, the difference between forward kinematics (joints to position) and inverse kinematics (position to joints), the URDF file that describes a robot to ROS, and the design process that takes a use case and turns it into hardware. If you've never picked up a textbook on robot mechanics, start here.

sections.heading: Rigid-body mechanics - the assumptions everything builds on
sections.body: A robot link is modelled as a rigid body - infinitely stiff, mass concentrated through the body, characterised by mass m, centre-of-mass position, and an inertia tensor I (3×3 matrix describing how the mass resists rotation). Every dynamics calculation downstream - Newton-Euler, Lagrangian, the dynamics term in MoveIt - assumes this rigid-body abstraction. Real bodies flex, but for arms moving slowly enough the approximation is excellent.
bullets: Mass m - scalar; how much force it takes to accelerate the link
Inertia tensor I (3×3) - how torque maps to angular acceleration. Diagonal in body's principal-axis frame
Centre of mass - location where applied forces produce no rotation
Wrench (force + torque, 6-vector) - the canonical action acting on a body
Twist (linear + angular velocity, 6-vector) - the canonical motion of a body
Newton's 2nd: F = m·a translation; τ = I·α + ω × (I·ω) for rotation

sections.heading: Joints & actuators - the moving parts
sections.body: A joint connects two links and allows constrained relative motion. Five canonical types cover almost every robot you'll see. Behind each joint sits an actuator that produces motion - choosing the right actuator dominates a robot's mass, speed, precision, and cost. Modern industrial arms use brushless DC motors with harmonic drives; humanoids increasingly use quasi-direct-drive (low-gear-ratio) BLDC for compliance.
bullets: Revolute - 1 DOF, rotational. The default for arm + leg joints
Prismatic - 1 DOF, linear. Linear stages, Z-axis lifts, Cartesian robots
Helical - 1 DOF, screw motion (rotation + translation locked). Rare in robotics
Spherical - 3 DOF, ball joint. Wrists, hip joints in humanoids
Universal - 2 DOF, two perpendicular revolute axes. Camera gimbals
Actuator types: BLDC + harmonic drive (industrial), QDD BLDC (humanoid), stepper (cheap), servo (hobby), hydraulic (heavy), pneumatic (soft)

codeBlocks.title: URDF: revolute joint with limits
codeBlocks.code:
<joint name="shoulder" type="revolute">
  <parent link="base_link"/>
  <child  link="upper_arm"/>
  <origin xyz="0 0 0.1" rpy="0 0 0"/>
  <axis xyz="0 0 1"/>
  <limit lower="-2.96" upper="2.96" effort="87" velocity="2.17"/>
  <dynamics damping="0.5" friction="0.2"/>
</joint>

sections.heading: Forward kinematics - joint angles → end-effector pose
sections.body: Forward kinematics is the function f(q) → SE(3) that maps a vector of joint values q to the pose (position + orientation) of the end-effector. There are two canonical conventions for writing this down: Denavit-Hartenberg (DH) - four parameters per joint, multiply 4×4 transforms - and the modern product-of-exponentials (PoE) form using twists in the Lie algebra se(3). The hexapod widget below gives intuition: drag the joint angles and watch the foot move.
bullets: DH convention - 4 numbers (a, α, d, θ) per joint; product of per-joint 4×4 transforms
Product of exponentials (PoE) - body-frame screw motions; Modern Robotics convention
End-effector pose: T = T₁ · T₂ · ... · Tₙ - order matters (right-to-left composition)
Singular configurations: where the Jacobian loses rank → infinite IK solutions or none
Workspace = the set of reachable end-effector poses - usually a torus / sphere with holes

codeBlocks.title: FK for a 2-link planar arm (numpy)
Same FK in C++ with Eigen - the production-grade pattern

sections.heading: Inverse kinematics - pose → joint angles
sections.body: IK is the harder direction: given a desired end-effector pose, find joint angles that achieve it. Some kinematic chains (6-DOF arms with a spherical wrist) admit closed-form analytical solutions. Most need iterative numerical solvers - typically damped least-squares using the Jacobian, or higher-quality solvers like TRAC-IK and BiO-IK that handle joint limits and singularities gracefully. Drag the red target on the widget below to see live IK on a 2-link planar arm.
bullets: Analytical IK - closed form, instant, geometry-specific (Pieper's solution for spherical-wrist 6-DOF)
Numerical IK - Newton-Raphson, damped least-squares (DLS); works for any chain
Redundant arms (≥7 DOF) - many solutions per pose; pick by secondary objective (avoid joint limits, max manipulability)
Modern solvers: KDL (default in MoveIt), TRAC-IK (better near limits), BiO-IK (handles arbitrary cost terms)
ML-based IK - neural networks trained on FK data; fast at inference, no convergence guarantee

codeBlocks.title: Damped least-squares IK - the workhorse
codeBlocks.code:
import numpy as np

def ik_step(J, err, lam=0.05):
    """One DLS step: dq that nudges the end-effector along err.
       J is the 6×n geometric Jacobian. err is a 6-vector (linear + angular)."""
    n = J.shape[1]
    return np.linalg.solve(J.T @ J + lam * lam * np.eye(n), J.T @ err)

# Iterate until ||err|| < tol or step count exhausted.
# In practice always check: joint limits, self-collision, manipulability.

sections.heading: Robot dynamics - torques and accelerations
sections.body: Dynamics is the relationship between torques applied at joints and the resulting accelerations of the chain. The standard formulation is M(q)q̈ + C(q,q̇)q̇ + g(q) = τ, where M is the joint-space inertia matrix, C captures Coriolis + centrifugal effects, g captures gravity, and τ is the joint-torque vector. The recursive Newton-Euler algorithm computes this in O(n) for n joints; the Lagrangian form is mathematically cleaner but slower to evaluate. Knowing dynamics matters for: torque-controlled motors, MPC, gravity compensation, and any contact-rich task.
bullets: M(q) - joint-space inertia matrix (positive definite, n×n)
C(q, q̇)q̇ - Coriolis and centrifugal forces; depend on velocity
g(q) - gravity-compensation torques; only depend on configuration
τ_friction - viscous + Coulomb; not in the canonical equation but always there in practice
Inverse dynamics - given (q, q̇, q̈), compute τ. RNEA = Recursive Newton-Euler
Forward dynamics - given (q, q̇, τ), compute q̈. Articulated-Body Algorithm in O(n)

codeBlocks.title: Use Pinocchio for fast rigid-body dynamics
codeBlocks.code:
import pinocchio as pin
import numpy as np

model = pin.buildModelFromUrdf('panda.urdf')
data  = model.createData()

q    = pin.neutral(model)
v    = np.zeros(model.nv)
a    = np.zeros(model.nv)

# Inverse dynamics: torques to produce desired joint accelerations
tau = pin.rnea(model, data, q, v, a)

# Joint-space inertia matrix
M = pin.crba(model, data, q)

# Gravity-compensation torques (set v = a = 0)
g = pin.computeGeneralizedGravity(model, data, q)

sections.heading: Coordinate frames & rotations
sections.body: Robotics is full of frames - world, base_link, camera, end-effector - and you constantly need to express points and poses in different ones. The math: rotation matrices SO(3), Euler angles (intuitive but suffer gimbal lock), axis-angle, and quaternions (4 numbers, no singularities, the de-facto standard in code). Drag the rotation widget below to see all three representations stay synchronised; the gimbal-lock preset shows the singularity that makes Euler dangerous near pitch ±90°.
bullets: Rotation matrix R: orthonormal 3×3, det(R) = +1; composes by multiplication
Euler ZYX (yaw-pitch-roll) - intuitive but gimbal-locks at pitch = ±90°
Axis-angle (ω̂, θ) - exponential coordinates of SO(3); easy interpretation
Quaternion q = (w, x, y, z) - singularity-free, slerp-friendly, the standard in code
Lie group SE(3) = rotation + translation; Lie algebra se(3) = twists
ROS 2 TF2: timestamped tree of named frames; lookup_transform interpolates

sections.heading: URDF / xacro - describing the robot to ROS
sections.body: URDF (Unified Robot Description Format) is the XML format every ROS tool reads. It lists every link (rigid body) and joint (connection), plus visual meshes, collision meshes, inertial properties. xacro is the macro language you'll actually write - it adds parameters, conditionals, and includes so URDFs don't become 5000-line copy-paste pyramids. SDF is the Gazebo-native sibling format; ros_gz translates between them.

codeBlocks.title: Tiny xacro that generates a 2-link arm
codeBlocks.code:
<?xml version="1.0"?>
<robot xmlns:xacro="http://www.ros.org/wiki/xacro" name="two_link">
  <xacro:property name="L1" value="0.4"/>
  <xacro:property name="L2" value="0.3"/>

  <xacro:macro name="link_cyl" params="name length">
    <link name="${name}">
      <visual>
        <origin xyz="0 0 ${length / 2}"/>
        <geometry><cylinder length="${length}" radius="0.025"/></geometry>
      </visual>
      <inertial>
        <mass value="1.0"/>
        <inertia ixx="0.01" iyy="0.01" izz="0.001" ixy="0" ixz="0" iyz="0"/>
      </inertial>
    </link>
  </xacro:macro>

  <link name="base_link"/>
  <xacro:link_cyl name="upper_arm" length="${L1}"/>
  <xacro:link_cyl name="forearm"   length="${L2}"/>

  <joint name="shoulder" type="revolute">
    <parent link="base_link"/><child link="upper_arm"/>
    <axis xyz="0 0 1"/>
    <limit lower="-3.14" upper="3.14" effort="10" velocity="1"/>
  </joint>

  <joint name="elbow" type="revolute">
    <parent link="upper_arm"/><child link="forearm"/>
    <origin xyz="0 0 ${L1}"/>
    <axis xyz="0 1 0"/>
    <limit lower="-2.0" upper="2.0" effort="10" velocity="1"/>
  </joint>
</robot>

codeBlocks.title: Render xacro → URDF and visualize
codeBlocks.code:
ros2 run xacro xacro two_link.urdf.xacro > two_link.urdf
ros2 launch urdf_tutorial display.launch.py model:=$(pwd)/two_link.urdf

sections.heading: End-effectors - the working business end
sections.body: The end-effector is what actually does the task. The choice dominates everything downstream (planning, perception requirements, success rate). Major families: parallel-jaw grippers (cheap, robust, the manipulation default), suction cups (flat surfaces, food/packages - Amazon Robotics relies heavily here), anthropomorphic hands (dexterous but expensive - Shadow Hand, Allegro), soft grippers (compliant, food-safe, low precision), and specialty tools (welding torches, paint sprayers, surgical instruments). Choosing well makes the rest of the project tractable; choosing poorly forces ugly software workarounds.
bullets: Parallel jaw - Robotiq 2F-85, Schunk EGP, OnRobot RG2; the default
Suction - vacuum-cup, multi-cup; great for warehouse picking, breaks on porous objects
Anthropomorphic - Shadow C5, Allegro V4, Inspire - 16+ DOF, ~$100K+
Soft / underactuated - Festo, Soft Robotics Inc; safe for irregular objects
Force / torque sensor at wrist (ATI, Robotiq FT300) - needed for assembly + impedance control
Tool changers - robot can swap end-effectors mid-task; common in assembly cells

sections.heading: The mechanical design process - use case → CAD → prototype
sections.body: Building a robot from scratch is a structured engineering process, not a vibe. The standard arc: requirements (payload, reach, speed, accuracy, environment) → concept design → kinematic synthesis (pick joint topology) → CAD modelling (SolidWorks, Onshape, Fusion 360) → simulation (FEA for stress, dynamics in Gazebo or Drake) → prototyping (3D print first, machine for production) → calibration → handoff to controls. Modern shortcut: start from an existing reference design (e.g., open-source quadruped, ROS-Industrial UR retrofit) and customize.
bullets: Requirements first: what payload, what reach, what cycle time, what env (IP rating, EMI)
CAD: SolidWorks (industry standard), Onshape (cloud), Fusion 360 (cheaper)
FEA - finite element analysis for stress, deflection, fatigue (SolidWorks Simulation, Ansys)
3D-print prototype in PETG/ABS for fit; machine in aluminum for production
URDF should match CAD exactly - automate export with the URDF Exporter SolidWorks plugin
Tolerance stack-up - small mechanical errors compound; calibrate post-assembly
Open hardware: Open Dynamic Robot, MIT Mini Cheetah, B1 (Unitree open papers)
